summary:
The transfer of full sovereignty over the Panama Canal Zone from the United States to Panama was completed on 31 December 1999, in accordance with the Torrijos-Carter Treaties signed in 1977. Since its construction and opening in 1914, the canal had been operated by the United States under terms that gave Washington jurisdiction over a ten-mile-wide zone bisecting the country. The treaties, negotiated under pressure from Panamanian nationalist sentiment and signed by President Jimmy Carter and Panamanian leader Omar Torrijos, set a timetable for gradual transfer of administrative control, with full sovereignty to pass on 31 December 1999.

The handover was controversial in the United States, where critics argued that the canal — strategically and commercially vital as a transit route linking the Atlantic and Pacific — should remain under American control. The Senate ratified the treaties by a narrow margin in 1978. Panama subsequently managed the canal transition successfully, and the Autoridad del Canal de Panamá has operated the waterway competently since taking full control.

The transfer symbolized the broader retreat of American gunboat diplomacy in Latin America and was a landmark moment in the relationship between the United States and Latin American nations. Panama has since expanded the canal significantly, with a major expansion completed in 2016 allowing transit of larger vessels.
